title: It Was Explained I Would Be a Lawyer
summary: Interviewed at his New Orleans home with his wife Anne, Richard Sobol sketches growing up on the west side of Manhattan, his lawyer father and mathematics-teacher mother, the Bronx High School of Science, Union College, and Columbia Law School, where he wrote a published note on the Supreme Court's Alabama gerrymander decision.

title: Stars in My Eyes
summary: Sobol traces his path from clerking for Judge Paul R. Hays on the Second Circuit to a stint at the Federal Trade Commission and an associate's job at Arnold, Fortas & Porter in 1963, drawn by Abe Fortas's right-to-counsel case Gideon, only to find the firm quite different from his idealistic expectations.

title: Assigned to New Orleans
summary: Turned down in 1964 for lack of a Southern bar admission, Sobol describes meeting Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee director Henry Schwarzschild that winter and being sent in August 1965 for a three-week volunteer stint, disappointed to draw New Orleans rather than Selma or Jackson, a letter he says changed his life.

title: Relief in Eight Days
topic: A School Case Before Judge West
summary: Sobol recounts filing a West Feliciana Parish school desegregation case before the racist Judge E. Gordon West in Baton Rouge, its dismissal the next day, and the Fifth Circuit's summary reversal a week later, convincing him the South needed permanent lawyers rather than vacationing volunteers.

title: A Permanent Lawyer in New Orleans
summary: He describes befriending the Collins, Douglas, and Elie firm, the most important civil rights firm in Louisiana and counsel to the Congress of Racial Equality, and how Schwarzschild raised the money over the winter for Sobol to return in summer 1966 as the permanent New Orleans lawyer, his early contact with Bogalusa figures Bob Hicks, Gayle Jenkins, and A.Z. Young.

title: A Bottle of Scotch in the Bottom Drawer
topic: The Black Lawyers of New Orleans
summary: Sobol portrays the three Black attorneys, Robert F. Collins, later the first Black federal judge in the South, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie, scraping out a living on accident and bankruptcy work in a poor neighborhood while serving CORE, gathering each evening in Collins's office for a drink and to talk over the day's work.

part: The Hottest City in the State
title: Hot and Smelly From the Paper Mill
summary: He describes Bogalusa's nightly mass meetings and Main Street marches against store owners, the focus on Crown Zellerbach as Title VII took effect, and his first appearance before the town's angry, crutch-wielding city judge, who slammed his crutch on the table when Nils Douglas introduced Sobol as coming from the firm of the newest Supreme Court nominee.

title: The File Cards Were Four Inches Thick
summary: Sobol tells of moving down with his wife and two children despite a bomb thrown at the Collins firm's hallway, working through a thick stack of pending-case file cards built by volunteer Jerry Gutman, and persuading Schwarzschild that hiring a local lawyer like Tulane graduate Don Juneau was cheaper than flying volunteers in.

title: Starting Over on a Blank Sheet
topic: CORE, LDF, and the EEOC Charge
summary: He distinguishes the CORE legal network, funded through New York and Carl Rachlin, from the NAACP Legal Defense Fund, and describes rewriting the rushed July 1965 Crown Zellerbach EEOC charge to spell out testing, seniority, and segregated-local-union practices that became prominent issues.

part: Three Who Would Not Give an Inch
title: It Became Active Because of Them
summary: Sobol attributes Bogalusa's intensity to three committed local leaders, a unionized Crown Zellerbach workforce accustomed to organization, and a San Francisco support group, the company's home city, that sent money and flew each leader west, and calls it the hardest-line segregationist city he saw, recalling that decades later in the 1990s the mayor still would not grant Black police officers modest requests like posting training opportunities.

title: She Wanted a Man to Lead It
summary: Sobol credits Gayle Jenkins as the spark of the Bogalusa Voters League who, by her account, recruited the outspoken A.Z. Young to lead it because she felt a man would be more effective, and brought in her cousin Bob Hicks and his wife, all of whom played significant roles.

title: Rifles on the Roof
summary: He describes the Hicks home on what became Robert Hicks Avenue as the hub for everyone coming to Bogalusa, where James Farmer and CORE's Lincoln Lynch visited, recounts the men who guarded the roof with rifles against threats, and contrasts the frontman A.Z. Young with the detail-minded Hicks who became plaintiff in many suits.

title: Consolidated With the Government
summary: With additional plant cases filed and the Free Southern Theater and SNCC Freedom Singers passing through, Sobol describes bringing the Crown Zellerbach case to trial in 1967, consolidated with United States versus Local 189, applying the methodical discovery, depositions, and document demands he had learned doing antitrust work, with George Cooper researching employment testing.

title: A Two-Day Hearing on the Prom Queen
summary: Sobol recounts how unexpectedly sympathetic Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe handled all three Bogalusa employment cases and the school case, treating even a two-day hearing on prom-queen selection seriously, and praises a Fifth Circuit that gave litigants what they were entitled to overnight, conditions he credits more than any special skill.

title: There Were Black Jobs and White Jobs
summary: He recalls drawing on the Virginia precedent Quarles versus Philip Morris and learning from Department of Justice lawyers Robert Moore and David Rose how seniority systems harmed Black employees barred from white jobs, so that by trial in the Local 189 case Judge Heebe issued an injunction and preliminary judgment.

title: Freedom of Choice Was Token
summary: Sobol describes the most token desegregation under freedom of choice, where a few Black children pushed by their parents entered white schools, the dilapidated Black school buildings and underpaid teachers he saw firsthand, and the office's drive to abolish freedom of choice, first ordered in Washington Parish by Judge Heebe.

title: Two or Three Days, Not Twenty-Five Years
summary: Granted a court order to view the plant, Sobol recounts seeing that the corrugated-board jobs were simple and repetitive, learnable in days rather than the twenty-five-year wait the company demanded, while the highest-paid Black job sat a notch below the lowest white job on a pay scale that climbed six or seven steps.

title: I Never Slept in Bogalusa
summary: He explains the overlapping sex and race discrimination across the box plant, bag plants, and paper mill, where women worked only in the all-white bag plants, and tells of his rule never to sleep in Bogalusa or such towns, crossing into Natchez or Vicksburg out of a fear of violence.

title: An Unorthodox View of the Deacons
summary: Sobol offers a skeptical account of the Deacons for Defense, saying he never heard the name while working in Bogalusa and saw no guns, only friends of the Hicks family guarding the roof with rifles, a view he says Lolis Elie shares, suggesting the Deacons story is attractive to historians.

title: Troopers Between the Marchers
summary: He recalls Klan violence he heard of but rarely saw, the earlier Hicks against Knight injunction against Bogalusa police concluded the day he arrived, and the march to Baton Rouge through Livingston Parish, the Klan's reputed headquarters, where guardsmen lined the route and soda bottles flew over the police.

part: A Library on the Counsel Table
title: He Opened the Trunk of Books
summary: Returning to the 1967 trial before Judge Heebe, Sobol describes George Cooper arriving with a camp trunk full of books, piling them on the counsel table and confronting each Crown witness with their own prior writings on testing, and the charts they made on the seniority issue, with Bob Hicks at his side through eight days as Bogalusa workers filled the courtroom.

title: What Is RSVP
summary: He recaps the Wonderlic test, a short general-knowledge exam never required of incumbent white workers until the Civil Rights Act, whose questions like the meaning of RSVP carried cultural bias, disqualifying eight of ten Black applicants in the first half hour, with no demonstrated job-relatedness to making paper.

title: Fifteen Promotions to the Top
summary: Sobol explains how Crown Zellerbach merged the racially labeled progression lines by pay, leaving all Black jobs at the bottom under a job-seniority system, so a Black worker could enter only the lowest white job and inch up vacancy by vacancy, the remedy of promotion by plant seniority for any qualified bidder, and the Justice Department lawyers' further idea of eliminating the line of progression entirely, over the defense that a collective bargaining agreement could not be illegal.

title: Job Assignments by Lottery
topic: The Seniority Theory and the Article
summary: Sobol gives his second seniority example, the all-white New Orleans transit system, where his case Fagan versus NOPSI before Judge Heebe won assignment of shifts by lottery, then the theory that a seniority system could itself be discriminatory by its impact, and the article he co-wrote with his Washington friend George Cooper, rearranged and published by the Harvard Law Review as one of the most cited in years, until the Supreme Court ruled the other way in the Teamsters case.

title: This Traitorous Thing
summary: Sobol recounts moving to Washington, briefly teaching, then later representing unions in Title VII cases, where the AFL-CIO establishment still resented him for the article they saw as betrayal, and reflects on critics who said he had polluted scholarship to advance civil rights, a charge he partly grants.

title: It's All How the Judge Feels
summary: He argues that civil rights outcomes turned on which judge heard the case, recalling weekends at judges' homes and July 4, 1967, at Judge Herbert W. Christenberry's French Quarter house as the judge phoned Bogalusa threatening to send marshals if jailed clients were not released, and laments today's hostile New Orleans federal bench.

title: A Special Place With the Alligators
topic: Leander Perez and the Battery Charge
summary: Sobol opens the Gary Duncan story with Leander Perez, the racist Plaquemines Parish boss who controlled the judges and threatened to jail activists with the alligators, against whom Judge Christenberry had ordered desegregation, and recounts how nineteen-year-old Duncan, intervening as his nephews faced four white boys on a roadside, touched one on the elbow and told him to go on, was reported as having slapped him, and was charged with battery before being sent to Sobol by the Justice Department.

title: Practicing Law Without a License
summary: Sobol describes challenging Louisiana's denial of jury trials for crimes punishable by two years or less, the conviction and sixty-day sentence before Perez's appointed judge, his exhaustion of remedies through the Louisiana Supreme Court, and his arrest by Perez and a sheriff for practicing law without a license as he left after securing a stay.

title: A Hearing on the State of Law Practice
summary: He tells of being bailed out by Don Juneau, then filing to enjoin his prosecution and declare bans on outside civil rights lawyers unconstitutional, a case before a three-judge court including Judge Heebe that became a weeks-long, daily-reported trial in which the government, Perez, the state, and the Louisiana State Bar were all parties, the court ultimately finding Sobol was temporarily in the state on leave from Arnold, Fortas & Porter.

title: They Tried Duncan Again
summary: Sobol recounts the federal court's injunction aided by the government's intervention, then winning Duncan versus Louisiana in the Supreme Court, which held the Fourteenth Amendment requires jury trials in state cases for all but the most minor crimes, and Duncan versus Perez before Judge Cassibry, which enjoined the malicious reprosecution, a trifecta capped by Duncan becoming a shrimper and parish leader.

title: Ballots to the Bedside
topic: Discrimination in Fact in Madison Parish
summary: Sobol describes voting rights work after the Voting Rights Act brought federal registrars, the case of Madison Parish Voters League head Zelma Wyche, who narrowly lost a police-chief race after the clerk of court harvested some two hundred ballots from white nursing homes, and a later case where school-board candidate Harrison Brown was disconnected from the Democratic rooster-lever line, both set aside by Judge Dawkins under his novel discrimination-in-fact doctrine and rerun, with Brown winning his.

title: Turn Off Those Bulldozers
summary: Sobol tells how Bob Hicks spotted that planned Bogalusa public housing was placed deep in segregated neighborhoods, leading to a fair-housing suit before Judge Cassibry that halted the bulldozers, and how Hicks pursued every detail down to football players and cheerleaders in the case Hicks versus Weaver, never settling for half a loaf.

part: Welfare, Juries, and a Rule for the Nation
title: Charged With Welfare Fraud
summary: Sobol recounts how he, his wife Anne, and Lolis Elie defended some twenty women prosecuted for welfare fraud in New Orleans federal court, challenging the grand jury's racial and economic composition with computer analysis of food-stamp and voter tapes showing only thirty percent of food-stamp recipients were registered against seventy-eight percent of others.

title: Twenty Years Before Batson
summary: He describes the prosecutor striking every Black juror with peremptory challenges, and convincing Judge Alvin B. Rubin that any reason but race was permissible, yielding the decision United States versus McDaniels, a holding the Supreme Court adopted nationally two decades later in Batson versus Kentucky.

title: A Schedule of Thursday and Friday
summary: Sobol lays out his chronology after LCDC: to Washington and the Institute for Policy Studies, a disliked semester teaching at the University of Michigan Law School while still flying to Louisiana for ongoing cases, two years with Marian Wright Edelman at the Washington Research Project, and a 1971 return to Louisiana with Anne.

title: The White Hauler Took the Profit
summary: He tells of sweet-potato farmers near Opelousas who, with CORE worker John Zippert and Father Albert J. McKnight, formed a cooperative to process and sell their own crop, resented by white haulers, whose office the district attorney had police raid, leading Sobol to win a federal injunction protecting the Southern Consumers Cooperative.

title: He Did Go to the Wedding
summary: Sobol recounts befriending John Zippert through the cooperative case, and how, when the clerk of court refused Zippert a marriage license to wed Carol because the couple was mixed-race, he walked in with a suit to compel it just after the Supreme Court decided Loving versus Virginia, and got to attend the happy wedding.

title: It Was Like a Contagion
summary: Reflecting at the interview's close, Sobol observes that even the short-term volunteer lawyers and law students he once doubted went home transformed into public service work, a contagion he caught in a very big dose alongside his anti-war involvement, so that after two years he could not return to commercial antitrust work, and the two union lawyers gave him a paying client that let him sustain a civil rights practice on his own terms.
